data.title: How the global grey market in SIM cards powers fraud and influence
data.description:
A massive international grey market in mobile phone SIM cards is fuelling large-scale manipulation and fraud online, a University of Cambridge study has found.
The researchers say physical and virtual SIMs from providers like SMSActivate, 5Sim, SMShub and SMSPVA were being used to verify fake accounts on social media platforms and e-commerce services.
“We find a thriving underground market through which inauthentic content, artificial popularity and political influence campaigns are readily and openly for sale,” said Jon Roozenbeek, the study’s co-leader.
Many online platforms require SMS verification when a new account is created, a security measure intended to confirm authenticity and curb the mass creation of fake profiles.
In principle, the check is meant to ensure a human was setting up an account at a service like WhatsApp, Telegram, Facebook, X, Shopify or Amazon.
With grey-market SIMs, however, shady organisations were allowing armies of online bots to be verified so they can masquerade as humans.

Criminals and opaque political actors then use these fake accounts to make their online presence look larger than it is, inflating metrics such as likes, followers and shares to make accounts appear more popular.
Grey-market SIMs were also used to create accounts that post rage bait and provocative content designed to trigger strong reactions and comments. Illicit campaigns like these are often mounted to manufacture trends intended to influence voters and social media users
The Cambridge team found that prices for fake SIM verifications vary widely by application and the purported origin country of the SIM. WhatsApp was the most expensive at an average of US$1.02 per verification, followed by Telegram at 89 US cents.
Verifications for platforms where users’ phone numbers were not visible, unlike WhatsApp and Telegram, were cheaper. Facebook, Grindr and Shopify averaged 8 US cents per verification. X and Instagram were 10 US cents, TikTok and LinkedIn 11 US cents, and Amazon 12 US cents.
To shed light on the trade, the researchers developed the Cambridge Online Trust and Safety Index (COTSI), which tracks daily prices for SMS verifications for 197 countries and more than 500 platforms.
Using COTSI data, available free at cotsi.org, the researchers examined whether and how the market responds to political events.

Analysing price data for eight platforms (Google/YouTube/Gmail, Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X, WhatsApp, TikTok, LinkedIn and Telegram) ahead of 61 national elections, they found SMS verification prices for Telegram and WhatsApp rose noticeably in the 30 days before a national vote.
Telegram verifications increased by an average of 12 per cent and WhatsApp by 15 per cent. Prices for the other six platforms remained stable.
Costs also depend on SIM origin country. Verifications using SIMs from Japan averaged US$4.93, followed by Australia at US$3.24, Turkey at US$2.54 and Malta at US$2.18.
Cheaper sources included the United States at 26 US cents, Britain at 10 US cents and Russia at 8 US cents per verification. Germany sat in the middle at an average of 63 US cents.
The study called for debate on whether mass acquisition and use of SIM cards should be made more difficult. It noted that since April in Britain the operation of so-called SIM farms without a legitimate reason has been banned.
These devices can hold dozens to hundreds of SIMs at once, enabling mass SMS sending, rapid number switching and large-scale verification to create many online accounts or blast out phishing messages.
The Cambridge team also urged platforms to make the origin country of the SIM used in verification more transparent.
At services such as Google/YouTube/Gmail, Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X, TikTok and LinkedIn, the country where an account is registered is generally not visible to other users, whereas for messaging apps it is easy to see where an account originates.

data.title: China’s Nvidia warns of overheating risks after 723% share surge
data.description:
A more than eight-fold surge in the share price of Chinese graphics processing unit (GPU) maker Moore Threads Technology has prompted the newly listed company to warn investors that the gains may have run ahead of fundamentals.
The Beijing-based chipmaker said in a statement to the Shanghai Stock Exchange on Friday that its shares could face the risk of declines after the rapid rally. There was no catalyst that needed to be disclosed and no material change in the company’s fundamentals, it said.
The warning came just a week after Moore’s high-profile debut on December 5, when the stock jumped 425 per cent to become the second-best performing new listing in Shanghai this year in terms of first-day trading. Despite the outsized gain, the shares climbed another 57 per cent through Thursday. In total, they had surged 723 per cent from the offer price of 114.28 yuan.
“The company’s shares have had a big swing in recent days,” Moore said. “There may be a situation of overheating on the market and irrational speculation. Investors should be well aware of the trading risk and make decisions prudently and investments rationally.”

The shares tumbled by as much as 19 per cent to 758 yuan on Friday on the technology-heavy Star Market.
Euphoric demand for Moore’s shares underscores a massive pivot by Chinese investors to tech stocks after Beijing pledged to pull out all the stops to achieve its goal of tech self-reliance. The strategy was prioritised at a key economic policy meeting that concluded on Thursday and in a critical five-year development plan approved in October by the ruling Communist Party.
Frenzied sentiment around Moore and other Chinese tech stocks also coincides with a global boom in artificial intelligence, with the Nasdaq 100 index refreshing new highs this year.
Dubbed as China’s Nvidia, Moore was founded in 2020 by James Zhang Jianzhong, who worked for the US chipmaker for 14 years. The Chinese company is also now focusing on AI accelerator chips for training large language models, moving away from low-end ones used for visual displays in games and other applications in its early days of business. It raked in 8 billion yuan (US$1.13 billion) from its initial public offering, the mainland’s second-largest stock sale this year.
In its exchange statement, Moore said its business outlook was uncertain, with new products yet to generate revenue. Sales would not be achieved until processes including product certification, customer guidance and mass shipments were completed, it said, adding that the company was lagging behind some global industry giants in research capability, accumulation of core technology and product ecosystems.
Moore also flagged the risks of above-industry-average valuations. Its shares traded at 1,009 times sales, compared with 11.8 times for other electronics makers, the statement said.
The company reported a loss of 724 million yuan in the first nine months of this year due to higher research and development spending. Sinolink Securities forecast that Moore would turn profitable in 2027.

data.title: Soft brain chip could help solve Neuralink’s big implant challenge
data.description:
Scientists have developed a new type of semiconductor that they hope will overcome a major problem for bioelectronic devices: the immune response that causes the body to reject implants.
Bioelectronics is the science of integrating electronic systems with biological systems using devices that can monitor or interact with the body’s natural processes.
Advanced examples include brain-computer interfaces, which translate brain signals into commands to control external devices.
Researchers in China and the United States, including the private company Neuralink, have taken invasive brain-computer interface technology to the clinical trial stage.
Since their findings were published in the peer-reviewed journal Science last month, the team from the University of Hong Kong said they had been contacted by medical and cell researchers to explore how they could use the discovery.
The team said they had developed 3D semiconductors in the form of hydrogels, moving beyond inflexible materials such as silicon so that scientists could create devices that were more compatible with our bodies.
Lead author Zhang Shiming, an assistant professor at HKU’s department of electrical and electronic engineering, said the hydrogel-based semiconductors were highly compatible with the body, with the comfortable feeling of a “second skin”.
Zhang said the technology had completed the proof-of-concept stage in the lab and was now at the fundamental research stage for use in areas such as brain-computer interface.
This could include areas such as deep brain stimulation, which involves implanting electrodes that deliver electrical currents, for example to stop epileptic seizures.
Another promising area is research from Elon Musk’s company Neuralink, which involves inserting multiple electrodes into various areas of the brain to collect signals from the brain and use them to control external devices.
“Since our ability to move is controlled by signals sent from the brain, if certain muscles are impaired, we can bypass that and control movement directly through the brain,” Zhang said.
“There would be many opportunities for medical and biological researchers to explore cutting-edge knowledge on this platform. It might help advance research in neuroscience and medicine.
“But the timeline for clinical applications will depend on the outcomes of these initial explorations, and we cannot make any definitive conclusions about that yet.”


Fitness trackers, wearable glucose meters and pacemakers are some of the most widely used examples of bioelectronic devices, while other potential uses for the 3D semiconductors include heart surgery, hardware that mimics how the human brain works, or soft robotic skin.
“Imagine a robot with soft, responsive skin. This skin could sense temperature and identify the materials it touches,” Zhang said.
“Even as a nonliving entity, the ability to provide electronic feedback could enhance the experience of human-machine interaction.”
He added: “Traditional semiconductors are made of 2D rigid, dry materials like silicon, which fundamentally mismatch with biological systems that are 3D, soft and wet.”
The new semiconductors “address the material mismatch by using biocompatible hydrogels as the base material instead of rigid silicon, which helps to suppress immune responses”.
However, they still maintain their semiconductor properties, such as the ability to switch on and off, and can be built on a nanoscale where necessary.
Zhang said the thick-gel electronics “enable a transition from 2D, rigid and loose brain-machine interfaces to 3D, soft and robust interfaces for more accurate brain signal recording, helping to understand neurodegenerative diseases”.
He said that using the semiconductors for brain-machine interfacing could “provide a new level of integration with living systems, such as cells, that inorganic materials like silicon and gold cannot achieve”.
Zhang added: “These biohybrid living transistors, due to the presence of cells, have the potential to suppress immune responses, allowing for prolonged implantation while maintaining signal amplification capabilities. This paves the way for advancing wearable and implantable healthcare device technologies.”
According to Zhang, hydrogels are mostly made of water – up to 99 per cent – with just 1 per cent of polymer solids functioning as semiconductors.
The material’s composition was similar to that of human skin, which is around 70 per cent water, making it so comfortable that the user would hardly notice its presence, he said.
“Hydrogels can be easily chemically modified to achieve desired properties for wearable comfort, such as breathability, softness and biocompatibility.”
When it came to implants, the devices “hold the potential to advance regenerative medicine, such as prosthetic cardiac tissues for heart repair”, he said.


Inflamed heart muscles can develop scarring, which increases the risk of heart failure or cardiac enlargement, which prevents the heart from pumping effectively.
But prosthetic cardiac tissue made from soft semiconductors could be implanted in the heart to repair its functions, Zhang said.
The 3D semiconductors could also serve as a platform for future energy-efficient computing hardware that mimics the workings of the brain.
He said real neurons were connected in 3D but the transistors in chips were only linked in 2D, limiting their efficiency.
“3D semiconductors enable 3D spatial connections, improving computing efficiency and reducing energy consumption. This could provide an alternative path to develop energy-efficient computing hardware for neuromorphic applications,” Zhang added.
He said 3D hydrogel semiconductors could enable more precise health monitoring and stimulation by bridging the gap between biomedical devices and biological systems.
“Our next steps include further understanding the science behind the materials, optimising device performance and ultimately delivering clinically transferable biomedical devices to meet pressing needs in hospitals such as wearable continuous monitoring, implantable stimulation and functional tissue repair.”
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: Najib’s wife cleared as Malaysia drops money laundering, tax evasion appeal
data.description:
Malaysian prosecutors have dropped their appeal against the acquittal of Rosmah Mansor, the wife of jailed former prime minister Najib Razak, in a case involving money laundering and tax evasion, saying they saw no prospect of success.
Since 2018, the couple have been subject to multiple corruption investigations surrounding Najib’s alleged role in the multibillion-dollar 1MDB scandal. Both have repeatedly denied all wrongdoing.
Rosmah, widely scorned in Malaysia for her extravagant lifestyle and penchant for luxury handbags, was acquitted of 12 money laundering charges and five tax evasion charges by the Kuala Lumpur High Court in December 2024.
The Attorney General’s Chambers initially decided to appeal the decision but withdrew its application on Tuesday, saying it could not definitively prove that Rosmah committed the offences.
“If the appeal proceeded, the prosecution would not be able to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt as required in a criminal trial, as key witnesses needed to prove the charges against the accused have died,” the AGC said in a statement late on Thursday.
Amer Hamzah Arshad, a lawyer for Rosmah, said on Friday that they welcomed the prosecution’s appeal withdrawal, adding that Rosmah “maintained her innocence throughout”.
The decision to withdraw the appeal does not affect other ongoing trials against Rosmah, the AGC said.


Rosmah is currently free on bail pending an appeal against a 10-year jail sentence in 2022 for soliciting and receiving bribes to help a company win a US$279 million solar power supply project from Najib’s government.
The AGC’s appeal withdrawal against Rosmah’s acquittal comes ahead of major court verdicts involving Najib, who is serving a six-year jail sentence after being found guilty of corruption and money laundering.
Najib’s conviction was one of several cases investigating his involvement in the alleged theft of billions of dollars from 1Malaysia Development Berhad, a state fund he helped establish in 2009 while he was prime minister.
The Kuala Lumpur High Court will decide on December 22 whether Najib can serve his sentence under house arrest. Just days later on December 26, it will deliver its verdict in the biggest trial that Najib faces concerning 1MDB.
Some 1MDB-linked charges against Najib have been dropped. He has denied all of the charges brought against him.

data.title: China woman with social anxiety gathers leftovers from 100 banquets to feed 120 stray cats
data.description:
A woman from southwestern China, who has been battling social anxiety, has gone viral after spending three years overcoming her fears by “crashing” around 100 wedding banquets of strangers to collect leftover food to feed over 120 stray cats.
The woman, known as Xuanxuan, hails from Baoshan, Yunnan province. Previously, she struggled with social anxiety while working part-time as a video editor.
In the winter of 2022, she began rescuing stray cats that she found shivering outside, and gradually, her home transformed into a shelter for more than 120 felines.

Xuanxuan highlighted that feeding just one small cat costs about 3 yuan (40 US cents) per day, which means her daily expenses can easily surpass 300 yuan.
The turning point occurred at a wedding she attended, where she observed that much of the untouched food was being discarded.
This experience inspired her to initiate a “Leftover Rescue Plan,” which involves collecting leftover food to nourish the hungry animals at her shelter.
Initially, approaching strangers was a challenge for her.

“I’ve always experienced a bit of social anxiety. Every time I open my mouth, it takes a lot of courage. But thinking of all the cats waiting at home motivated me to step out and face the public,” she shared with Cover News.
However, the response has been overwhelmingly positive. Most newlyweds have shown understanding and support, with many even enthusiastically inviting her to participate in the banquet.
As a gesture of gratitude, Xuanxuan often gifts 100 yuan (US$14) to the newlyweds, humorously referring to it as “a meal ticket from a kitten or puppy.”

Over the past three years, she has attended wedding banquets about every 10 days. At her busiest, she could bring home up to 30kg of clean, untouched leftover food, including chicken, sea bass, and shrimp.
Upon returning home, she would re-steam the food to lessen its oil and salt content before feeding it to the animals.
Her food collection efforts save her between 1,000 yuan (US$140) and 2,000 yuan each month. More importantly, they contribute to reducing food waste.
She also shares videos of herself packing leftover food at weddings on social media, resonating with many viewers and helping her amass over 1.5 million followers.

Recent clips have unexpectedly gone viral, with her most popular video receiving over 50 million views and more than 1.5 million likes.
“What I’m doing is meaningful—reducing waste and helping stray animals. If more people notice this, I hope it will inspire others to cherish food and care for stray animals,” she remarked.
She added that this “mission” has aided both her and her cats in healing and growing together.
“They’ve given me the courage to break out of my shell and engage in activities I never dared to pursue before.”

This remarkable story has sparked widespread discussion online, moving many individuals while also garnering strong support.
One commenter stated: “This is such a great initiative! There’s always so much leftover food after wedding banquets. It feels like such a waste. This way, it not only helps stray animals but also reduces waste. It should definitely be promoted.”
Another chimed in: “Amazing. Such a kind-hearted girl, showing so much love and care for stray animals.”

data.title: Hong Kong man remanded after ‘impersonating fire victim, claiming HK$18,000 in aid’
data.description:
A jobless Hong Kong man has been remanded in custody on suspicion of defrauding HK$18,000 (US$2,313) in financial aid intended for those affected by last month’s deadly Tai Po fire.
Kwai Chung resident Fung Chun-kit, 36, was not required to enter a plea during his first appearance at Fanling Court on Friday, as prosecutors sought extra time to investigate the case.
A charge sheet available for media inspection states that Fung successfully registered with the Social Welfare Department for emergency relief by impersonating a victim of the Wang Fuk Court inferno, which claimed at least 160 lives.
The defendant allegedly said that he was Chan Man-him, a resident of a fifth-floor flat at Wang Shing House, while the actual occupant was Wong Kam-hung.
Fung is accused of using the false identity to obtain a total of HK$18,000 in relief funds from the Home Affairs Department, the Lok Sin Tong Benevolent Society, Kowloon and Hong Kong Sheng Kung Hui – the city’s Anglican church – between November 29 and Tuesday.
When the accused was stopped by police shortly after receiving financial assistance from the church, he failed to show his identity card when asked by an officer, according to the court document.
Fung faces three counts of obtaining property by deception and one count each of fraud and failure to comply with the requirement to produce proof of identity for inspection.
Prosecutors told the court that investigators would check whether Fung had deceived any other government departments or non-profit groups, while gathering evidence from relevant surveillance footage and the defendant’s mobile phone.
Magistrate Kenneth Chan Ping-chau scheduled the next hearing for mid-February before denying Fung bail.
The fire that raged through seven of Wang Fuk Court’s eight blocks for 43 hours last month has so far killed 160 people and left almost 5,000 residents displaced.
Police have previously prosecuted two people for allegedly laundering a total of HK$115,000 bilked from online donors, who believed their money would be used to help those affected by the fire.
Mainland Chinese resident Tian Li, 27, and 32-year-old cook Cham Shu-shing, were both released on bail pending their respective trials.

data.title: Hong Kong ride-hailing car quota should reflect Uber’s operating ‘reality’: CEO
data.description:
The quota for Hong Kong’s ride-hailing vehicles to be set by the authorities should reflect Uber’s operating situation in the city, the firm’s CEO has urged, adding that the company’s focus on safety will help it stand out among competitors.
Dara Khosrowshahi, speaking to the media on Friday during a visit to Hong Kong, said the company “wouldn’t necessarily object” to a levy on ride-hailing trips to support the taxi trade similar to the system used in some parts of Australia, but the firm’s final stance would depend on the details of the regulatory framework.
“Just in the past year, tourists from over 100 countries have used us right here in Hong Kong, we have 30,000 Uber drivers on the road now, so we’re hoping that any initial quota actually reflects those initial realities,” he said.
“Obviously, not every single one of them is on the road at a particular time, but we’re hoping that the quota creates continuity in terms of the service and then also allows for growth going forward.”
Lawmakers earlier passed the Road Traffic (Amendment) Bill to legalise ride-hailing operations in Hong Kong. The government is expected to propose a regulatory framework early next year, with licences to be issued in the middle of 2026.
The licensing system will set vehicle age and insurance requirements, and ride-hailing platforms, drivers and vehicles will require separate licences and permits. Authorities plan to use subsidiary legislation to determine the number of ride-hailing vehicle licences to be issued.
Currently, it is illegal in Hong Kong for private vehicle drivers to accept paid customers without a hire-car permit, but ride-hailing platforms such as Uber, Tada, Amap and Didi Chuxing operate such services without regulation.
Amap is operated by Alibaba Group Holding, which also owns the South China Morning Post.
Citing the company’s average estimated time of arrival of five minutes for its vehicles, Khosrowshahi hoped that the quota set by authorities would help the company maintain the reliability of its service in Hong Kong.
“We would ask that the quota be dynamic as well. The majority of our drivers are part time; they work less than 20 hours a week. So the number of cars on the road is going to move up and down based on the time of day or the day of the week, based on demand patterns,” he said.
Khosrowshahi said he believed the company’s focus on safety would help it stand out among its competitors in Hong Kong’s ride-hailing market, adding that the firm had technology in place for tracking vehicles, vetting drivers and verifying that all documentation was in order.
“This is technology that we have built in the over 70 countries that we operate in. We think that in Hong Kong, we can lead in safety. As long as we deliver on our promises, we’re hoping that the rest can take care of itself,” he said.

Asked about the company’s view on a levy imposed on ride-hailing services to support the taxi trade such as that used in Australia, Khosrowshahi said Uber had worked with the Australian government on the issue and the system was something the company was familiar with.
He said the company “wouldn’t necessarily object” to a similar system in Hong Kong to compensate taxi drivers, but the firm’s final position would depend on the regulatory framework that authorities implement.
Citing suggestions that authorities should adopt a levy system similar to that of Australia, the Hong Kong government said in its initial proposal that it would consider imposing a levy on online ride-hailing platforms, provide resources to support the local taxi trade, enhance their service quality and promote the sector’s sustainable development.

data.title: Greek minister takes over as Eurogroup president, marking historic turnaround
data.description:
Greece’s finance minister was on Thursday named head of the Eurogroup, the body that helps shape euro zone policy, underlining his country’s turnaround since the years when it came close to crashing out of the single currency.
Kyriakos Pierrakakis saw off the rival Belgian candidate to become president of the assembly of finance ministers from the 20 countries sharing the euro.
Greece had to be bailed out three times between 2010 and 2015 by euro zone governments to avoid economic collapse after years of excessive borrowing and overspending.
It now has steady economic growth above 2 per cent, the highest investment rates in the euro zone, budget surpluses and public debt that, though still the highest in Europe, has been falling quickly for years.
“This election is an important symbolic moment for Greece and the euro area: we all remember the deep financial crisis that Greece had to go through and even questions were raised about Greece’s continued membership in the euro area,” European Economic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis told a news conference.
“Now we see a remarkable turnaround with Greece being among the euro area’s best performing economies with a fiscal surplus and now with a Greek finance minister as the president of the Eurogroup,” he said.

The Eurogroup gathers finance ministers of the 20 countries sharing the EU’s single currency who meet once a month ahead of the bigger, formal talks of all 27 EU finance ministers to discuss issues related to the euro, and the euro zone economy.
The 42-year-old computer scientist will also represent the single-currency area at G7 finance ministers’ meetings, shape the agenda of the monthly meetings and seek to work out compromises and common positions.
“Greece has come such a long way in the past 10 years,” European Council President Antonio Costa said on X.
“Your election today as President of the Eurogroup, dear Kyriakos Pierrakakis, is a meaningful recognition of that progress, both for your country and for our Union,” he said.
Pierrakakis had been vying for the post with Belgium’s deputy prime minister, Vincent Van Peteghem, since Paschal Donohoe resigned as Irish finance minister and Eurogroup President to take up a senior World Bank role.
He became Greek finance minister earlier this year.
In his previous role as digital governance minister of the centre-right government of Kyriakos Mitsotakis between 2019-2023, he spearheaded the digitisation of the state as Greece recovered from the 2009-2018 debt crisis.
Pierrakakis will take office on December 12 and serve a 2½-year term.

data.title: China’s desalination technology, Hong Kong Legco election turnout: SCMP’s 7 highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven stories from this week’s news across Hong Kong, mainland China, the wider Asia region and beyond that resonated with our readers and shed light on topical issues. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. China desalination tech makes fresh water cheaper than tap water – plus hydrogen
China has launched a revolutionary facility in the eastern province of Shandong that produces fresh water from seawater for just two yuan (US$0.28) per cubic metre, generating green hydrogen as a by-product in a breakthrough that could redefine global water and energy systems.
2. China ‘did tell Japan about air drills but not in enough detail to avoid danger’

Japanese Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi acknowledged that China had notified Japan in advance about drills involving the aircraft carrier Liaoning that led to a stand-off between warplanes from the two countries over the weekend.
3. Former Chinese senior banker executed for taking US$155 million in bribes

Bai Tianhui, the former general manager of the asset management firm China Huarong International Holdings, was executed on Tuesday after he was found guilty of taking more than 1.1 billion yuan (US$155 million) in bribes, state broadcaster CCTV reported.
4. Could ICC warrants against Duterte allies alter Philippines’ balance of power?
A former spokesman for Rodrigo Duterte has claimed that the International Criminal Court is preparing to arrest two of the ex-Philippine leader’s closest allies over his administration’s bloody war on drugs.
5. Hong Kong Legco election’s 31.9% turnout surpasses record low of 2021




Some 31.9 per cent of voters cast their ballots on Sunday in Hong Kong’s second Legislative Council election under a “patriots-only” system, beating the record low turnout of 2021 in a sombre poll held just 12 days after the tragic fire in Tai Po.
6. China ‘most beautiful’ bodyguard in spotlight during French president visit

A female bodyguard, assigned by the Chinese government to protect the French first lady during her official visit with her husband to China in early December, went viral on social media for her tough and professional demeanour.
7. Su Bingtian, first Chinese to run 100m under 10 seconds, says ‘goodbye, my dearest track’
China’s Su Bingtian, Asia’s fastest man, announced his retirement from athletics on Tuesday, saying his body had told him to “pass the baton”.

data.title: Kuala Lumpur restaurant brings Palestine’s rich flavours to Malaysia – with an extra kick
data.description:
A large mural stands at the entrance of Yafa Restaurant in Kuala Lumpur’s charming Taman Tun Dr Ismail township. In bold, graffitied letters inspired by art on the West Bank barrier – which divides the Israeli state from the western Palestinian territories – a slogan reads: “Make hummus not walls”.
Step past into the patio area and you will be greeted by the vibrant scents of cinnamon and wild thyme; walk a little further in and you will see wallpaper featuring pomegranates, oranges, poppies and patterns of the Palestinian keffiyeh in shades of olive.
Yafa Restaurant, which opened earlier this year, transports the rich flavours of Palestine to the Malaysian capital. The name references the ancient Palestinian port city of Yafa, known in English as Jaffa and located in modern-day Israel.
“My dad was one year old when the first Nakba happened,” says co-founder Ahmad Hallalo, referring to the 1948 mass expulsion and displacement of Palestinian Arabs when the Israeli state was established.
His father, originally from Yafa, migrated to Gaza before earning a scholarship to study in Lebanon and finally settling in Abu Dhabi to raise his family.
Hallalo’s journey eventually took him to Kuala Lumpur 17 years ago. It was here he met Zara Ahmad Riza, a Malaysian native, and their 13-year marriage led them to co-found the restaurant as partners in both life and business.
“Middle Eastern food is so delicious, so I’ve always liked it,” Riza says. “And because I’m a good daughter-in-law, I learned more about it.”

Today, Yafa has a 10-person team led by Riza and Hallalo. They are supported by Husnie Halim, the head of operations, who has many years of F&B experience.
The menu has delectable Palestinian dishes including maqluba, a traditional Levantine upside-down dish of meat and rice toasted with bay leaves and cinnamon; fattoush, a salad of mixed greens tossed with fried flat bread; and za’atar nanakeesh, typically a breakfast dish made from a blend of thyme, oregano and sumac spread on top of bread, often combined with akkawi, a white brined cheese named after the former Palestinian (now Israeli) city of Acre.
A honey and pistachio mocktail acts as a cooling refresher, while knafeh, layered pastry dough with cheese soaked in a sweet syrup, satisfies those sugar cravings.
Before opening Yafa, Riza established Marookha, a “gentlewoman’s club”, in 2021 after the Covid-19 pandemic left her without a job. Located above the current restaurant, it is a cafe-style space in which women can relax, attend art workshops, practise fitness and celebrate Ramadan in a safe and private environment.

To support the humanitarian crisis in Gaza, Marookha also hosted two fundraising events where they served Palestinian food.
“I started with a very small menu that I thought Malaysians would accept,” Riza says, initially concerned about the differing Malaysian and Palestinian palates.
She and Hallalo had no prior professional food and drink experience, so putting together a menu for Marookha was trial and error, she says.
Videos of their fundraising events quickly went viral – the men wanted a taste of the food, too. The success of Marookha gave the couple a new idea: to expand its menu into a full restaurant downstairs.
“When we finally opened [Yafa], we had a bigger team, and I wanted to choose the dishes that I liked even if Malaysians might not understand it,” Riza says. As Yafa developed, she became more emboldened to showcase Palestinian cuisine and all its complexities.
But the one thing she is willing to adapt is incorporating more chilli. “It has to be spicy,” she laughs, citing how Malaysians typically love their food to have a kick.


Riza’s role as a bridge between the two cultures – a role she also plays at home – is central to the restaurant’s identity.
Explaining how Hallalo’s family have accepted her as one of their own, she wanted this cosy, welcoming feeling to also be reflected in the restaurant.
“I intentionally decorated [Yafa] to make you feel like you’re in your grandma’s house,” she says. Pillows with traditional Bedouin al-sadu patterns welcome guests at a seating area, and the wallpaper features staple fruits and herbs of the land.
To Riza and Hallalo, food is far from just a meal on the table – it can be a form of resistance. Cuisine traditions embody a community’s history, environment and identity in a tangible, sensory way. For communities that have migrated, food is perhaps one of the most stable links to their homeland.
“How do you erase a people? You take their food, you change their clothes or you change their language,” says Riza. The menus, therefore, detail a brief history of Yafa, not only to honour Hallalo’s roots, but also to remember the displaced peoples of the city.
Further showcasing the spirit of Palestinian resistance, the mural on the patio area incorporates powerful symbolism, including an illustration of the iconic Handala, a boy with ragged clothes and his back turned, created by Palestinian newspaper cartoonist Naji al-Ali in 1969.
The mural has since become a hotspot for photographs, and the restaurant draws patrons from the Muslim Malay community as well as other Malaysians and tourists.

Looking ahead for Yafa, Hallalo and Riza hope to open new spin-off branches: one for more high-end ingredients, another offering cheaper, more accessible meals through a food truck.
Yafa and Marookha continue to be hotspots for Malaysians to support the Palestinian cause, with future plans to host tatreez embroidery workshops or engage with the Palestinian embassy for various events.
Hallalo says that, for them, the business is not just about generating profit.
“Are you impacting the lives of other people, or just making money to keep going in your life?”
